note_data.title: States of matter
keyPoints: **Kinetic theory** states that all particles are in constant random motion with kinetic energy proportional to absolute temperature
In **solids**, particles vibrate about fixed positions with strong intermolecular forces holding them in a regular lattice structure
In **liquids**, particles move randomly but remain close together with weaker intermolecular forces than solids
In **gases**, particles move rapidly in random directions with negligible intermolecular forces and occupy all available space
**Ideal gas** obeys all gas laws at all temperatures and pressures, with particles having no volume and no intermolecular forces
**Real gases** deviate from ideal behavior at high pressure and low temperature due to particle volume and intermolecular attractions
**Boiling point** is the temperature at which vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure and bubbles form throughout the liquid
Increase in pressure **raises boiling point** because vapor pressure must reach a higher value to equal atmospheric pressure
**pV = nRT** applies to ideal gases where p = pressure, V = volume, n = moles, R = gas constant, T = absolute temperature

definitions.definition: A gas that obeys the equation pV = nRT at all temperatures and pressures, with particles having negligible volume and no intermolecular forces
The temperature at which the vapor pressure of a liquid equals the atmospheric pressure and bubbles of vapor form throughout the liquid
The theory that all matter consists of particles in constant random motion with average kinetic energy proportional to absolute temperature
The pressure exerted by vapor molecules when the liquid and vapor are in dynamic equilibrium

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give observable changes or characteristics without explaining why
State similarities AND differences between the two states

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the boiling point of water increases when pressure is increased. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure. When atmospheric pressure increases, the vapor pressure must reach a higher value to equal it. This requires a higher temperature, so the boiling point increases.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure
Mark 2: Higher atmospheric pressure means vapor pressure must reach higher value
Mark 3: Higher temperature needed, so boiling point increases

commonMistakes: Never say 'molecules have more energy' — always say 'average kinetic energy increases'
Students often confuse vapor pressure with atmospheric pressure in boiling point explanations
Never say 'particles get bigger' when heated — say 'particles move faster and further apart'
Don't confuse evaporation with boiling — evaporation occurs at surface only, boiling occurs throughout liquid
Never use Celsius in gas law calculations — must convert to Kelvin (K = °C + 273)
examinerTips: Gas law questions appear in every Paper 2 — practice unit conversions
Always state assumptions when using ideal gas equation (negligible intermolecular forces, negligible particle volume)
Pressure cookers in Pakistani kitchens are good examples of increased pressure raising boiling point
Draw particle diagrams showing relative spacing and motion for different states
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Particle arrangement diagrams showing regular lattice structure in solids, close but random arrangement in liquids, and widely spaced random arrangement in gases with motion arrows
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q2 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the changes in particle arrangement and movement when ice melts and then boils, including energy changes
N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — a multiple choice question asking which property increases from solid to liquid to gas: particle speed, particle mass, or particle size
M/J 2022 P2 Q4 (5 marks) — students had to describe diffusion in gases using kinetic particle theory and explain why it occurs faster at higher temperatures
N 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — required explanation of why solids have fixed shape but liquids take shape of container, using particle theory

workedExample.question: Explain, in terms of particle theory, why a solid has a fixed shape but a liquid does not. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: State the particle arrangement in solids - particles are closely packed in regular, fixed positions
Step 2: Explain particle movement in solids - particles can only vibrate about fixed positions, cannot move freely
Step 3: State the particle arrangement in liquids - particles are closely packed but in irregular, random positions
Step 4: Explain particle movement in liquids - particles can move freely past each other while remaining close together
workedExample.answer: In a solid, particles are closely packed in regular, fixed positions and can only vibrate about these fixed positions. This gives the solid a fixed shape. In a liquid, particles are closely packed but in irregular positions and can move freely past each other. This allows the liquid to flow and take the shape of its container.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for solid particle arrangement, 1 mark for solid particle movement, 1 mark for liquid particle arrangement, 1 mark for liquid particle movement

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused particle size with particle spacing' — always mention that particle size stays the same, only the spacing and movement change between states

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often wrote vague answers about particles moving faster without specifying the type of movement' — always specify vibration for solids, sliding for liquids, and random motion for gases

Examiner Report 2023: 'Students frequently forgot to mention energy when explaining state changes' — always include that heating provides energy to overcome forces between particles during melting and boiling
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw particle diagrams showing: (1) Solid - circles touching in regular rows/layers with small arrows showing vibration, (2) Liquid - circles touching but randomly arranged with curved arrows showing movement past each other, (3) Gas - circles far apart with long straight arrows in random directions. Include labels for 'strong forces', 'weak forces', and 'very weak forces' between particles.
note_data.quickRecap: Solids have particles in fixed positions that only vibrate, giving fixed shape and volume. Liquids have particles that move past each other, giving fixed volume but variable shape. Gases have particles moving randomly with large spaces between them, giving variable shape and volume. State changes occur when energy overcomes forces between particles.
